signals.headline: **Energy's Dominance:** The energy index rose 10
signals.raw:
**Energy's Dominance:** The energy index rose 10.9% in March alone, accounting for nearly three-quarters of the monthly headline increase.[1]

These figures would likely move markets by reinforcing expectations that the Federal Reserve will maintain higher interest rates for longer, affecting stock volatility, bond yields, and currency markets.

signals.headline: March 2026 CPI Surges to 3
signals.raw:
March 2026 CPI Surges to 3.3% on Historic Gas Price Spike | YES | HIGH confidence**

The Consumer Price Index for March 2026 jumped 0.9% month-over-month and 3.3% year-over-year, driven primarily by a 21.2% monthly surge in gasoline prices—the worst single-month gas increase since 1967.[1][2] This represents the largest monthly inflation gain since 2022, triggered by the US-Israel war and Iranian

signals.headline: **Federal Reserve Rate Cuts Delayed:** The inflation acceleration has pushed rate cut expectations further into the futu
signals.raw: **Federal Reserve Rate Cuts Delayed:** The inflation acceleration has pushed rate cut expectations further into the future. Most Fed policymakers still expect at least one rate cut in 2026, but a growing minority now see no cuts at all, with the Fed holding rates at 3.50%–3.75%.[2]

signals.headline: **Core Inflation Contained:** Stripping out volatile energy and food prices, core inflation remained relatively benign a
signals.raw: **Core Inflation Contained:** Stripping out volatile energy and food prices, core inflation remained relatively benign at 2.6% year-over-year and 0.2% month-over-month—slightly below economist expectations.[1][2] This suggests the inflation shock is primarily energy-driven rather than broad-based.
